structured_choices.data_source: Breast Cancer Surveillance Consortium (BCSC) dataset: 3D mammography images (tomosynthesis) with confirmed diagnostic labels from a 5-year period. Data sourced from multiple institutions to reflect real-world variability. Minimum 5,000 studies recommended (approximately 40% malignant, 60% benign based on typical BCSC prevalence).
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: malignancy status determined by pathology confirmation (gold standard) for biopsied lesions, or confirmed by imaging follow-up consensus for non-biopsied cases at 2+ years. Lesion-level labels are linked to images; studies with multiple lesions are handled as separate instances with appropriate identifiers to prevent leakage.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split at the patient level (not image level) to prevent information leakage from multiple views of the same patient appearing in train and test. Stratification by: (1) malignancy status (binary), (2) institution/site (to ensure each site represented proportionally in train/val/test), and (3) patient age decile (to control for demographic confounding). Split: 60% train (n≈3000 patients), 20% validation (n≈1000 patients), 20% held-out test (n≈1000 patients). Temporal ordering is NOT used as primary split criterion because the hypothesis compares CNN vs. radiologist on *the same data*, not on prospective deployment; however, image acquisition dates are logged to detect any temporal drift patterns post-hoc.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (intensity normalization, artifact detection thresholds, image resizing) are fit exclusively on the training set. Validation and test sets are transformed using the training set statistics (e.g., mean/std for normalization). Any data augmentation (rotation, elastic deformation) is applied only during training. Quality control filters (e.g., excluding unreadable images) are applied uniformly before any split, using site-agnostic criteria established a priori.
structured_choices.features: Raw input: 3D mammography volumes (tomosynthesis reconstructions, 512×512×64 voxels typical). Preprocessing: (1) standardize intensity to zero mean, unit variance using training set statistics; (2) resample to fixed 256×256×32 voxels to normalize across scanner differences; (3) flag and handle motion artifacts or low-signal studies via automated quality score (applied identically across all splits). No handcrafted radiomics features are engineered; the CNN operates end-to-end on preprocessed volumes. Metadata (age, BMI, prior imaging history) are not provided to the model, ensuring the comparison isolates image content understanding.
structured_choices.model: 3D convolutional neural network (CNN): ResNet50-style architecture adapted for 3D volumetric input (e.g., 3D residual blocks, 3D pooling). Input: 256×256×32 volumetric patch. Output: binary logit (sigmoid for probability). Hyperparameters (depth, learning rate, regularization, batch size) are tuned on the validation set using Bayesian optimization or grid search over a specified search space.
structured_choices.baseline: Radiologist assessments from the BCSC dataset: BI-RADS scores (1–5) converted to binary predictions (scores 4–5 = malignant, 1–3 = benign). Baseline is the *consensus assessment* of two independent radiologists (or the primary radiologist's assessment if consensus unavailable). This is a fair comparison because: (1) it represents the standard of care that the model must exceed; (2) radiologists have access to the same 3D images as the model; (3) no additional clinical history is provided to either radiologists or model. The baseline is *not* tuned or optimized; it reflects real-world radiologist performance as recorded in the database. A secondary baseline (logistic regression on tabular patient features: age, BMI, prior imaging history, if available) may be included to decompose whether improvements are due to image understanding vs. model expressiveness.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC) on the held-out test set. Justification: (1) AUC-ROC is threshold-agnostic and summarizes performance across all operating points, appropriate because the stakeholder has not specified a fixed sensitivity/specificity target a priori; (2) it is invariant to class imbalance (important here given ~40% prevalence) and does not require choosing a decision threshold before deployment; (3) AUC-ROC allows comparison with radiologist performance via sensitivity/specificity at clinically relevant thresholds. A 95% confidence interval on AUC-ROC will be computed via bootstrap (1000 resamples) to quantify uncertainty.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (true positive rate) at the operating point where the model's specificity equals the radiologists' specificity on the test set. This operationalizes the stakeholder's concern: does the model catch more cancers (higher sensitivity) while maintaining equivalent false positive rates?
Specificity (true negative rate) at the operating point where the model's sensitivity equals the radiologists' sensitivity. Complementary view: does it reduce unnecessary procedures?

Calibration error (expected calibration error, ECE) on the test set, computed via binning predictions into 10 deciles and comparing predicted vs. observed malignancy rates. Critical for clinical deployment: poorly calibrated confidence scores could lead to inappropriate clinical decisions.

Per-site performance (AUC-ROC stratified by institution). This validates that the model generalizes across scanner types and imaging protocols, addressing the known confound of machine/site variability.
Per-demographic group performance (AUC-ROC stratified by age decile, BMI quartile). Checks for fairness and reveals whether the model exhibits disparate performance in subgroups.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (n≈1000 patients) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Early stopping is employed: train the model on the training set, evaluate on the validation set after each epoch, and halt training when validation AUC-ROC plateaus or degrades over 20 epochs. The final model weights (at the validation AUC-ROC maximum) are retained. The test set is not consulted during any model selection or tuning; it is opened only once, after model selection is finalized, to report final performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The held-out test set (n≈1000 patients) is used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, to report primary and secondary metrics. No hyperparameter adjustments, retraining, or threshold tuning are performed after test set evaluation. Results from the test set are reported with point estimates and 95% confidence intervals. If multiple CNN architectures are to be compared, each is tuned separately on the validation set using identical hyperparameter spaces; the best architecture is then selected based on validation AUC-ROC, and only that final model is evaluated on the test set.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound 1 — Demographic differences (age, BMI): Addressed via stratified splitting (patient-level stratification by age and demographics ensures proportional representation in train/val/test). Per-demographic secondary metrics reveal whether the model exhibits disparate accuracy across groups. Confound 2 — Institutional/equipment differences (scanner type, imaging protocol): Addressed via stratified splitting by site. Per-site secondary metrics quantify generalization. Confound 3 — Radiologist variability: Mitigated by using consensus assessment or primary radiologist as baseline (not a single radiologist's varying opinion). Confound 4 — Temporal drift in imaging standards: Logged but not used for splitting (since the comparison is cross-sectional). Post-hoc analysis checks whether model vs. radiologist performance diverges over the 5-year span. Confound 5 — Lesion characteristics (size, location) influencing both radiologist and model: Not explicitly adjusted; instead, the stratified random split ensures these characteristics are balanced across train/val/test. If known to be highly imbalanced, lesion-level stratification could be added. No data leakage through patient-level splitting, feature engineering on full data before splitting, or use of future information (follow-up imaging) in training labels.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Radiologist Assessment for Breast Cancer Malignancy Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Labels

This experiment uses the Breast Cancer Surveillance Consortium (BCSC) dataset, which contains 3D mammography images (tomosynthesis) acquired over a five-year period across multiple institutions. We will extract approximately 5,000 patient studies, with an anticipated prevalence of ~40% malignant and ~60% benign cases, reflecting real-world screening populations.

Labels are defined as confirmed malignancy status: pathology confirmation (via biopsy) serves as the gold standard. For non-biopsied lesions, consensus imaging follow-up assessment at 2+ years provides labeling. All labels are assigned at the patient study level before any data split, linked to 3D image volumes.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

To prevent information leakage and reflect real-world deployment constraints, we employ **stratified random splitting at the patient level** (not by individual images). Each patient's complete imaging study is assigned to exactly one set: train, validation, or test.

Stratification is performed on three dimensions:
- **Malignancy status** (binary): ensures both classes are represented proportionally in all sets.
- **Institution/site**: ensures each imaging facility is represented across train, validation, and test, preventing the model from learning site-specific imaging artifacts rather than cancer patterns.
- **Patient age decile**: controls for demographic confounding, ensuring age distributions are balanced.

Final split: **60% training** (n≈3,000), **20% validation** (n≈1,000), **20% held-out test** (n≈1,000).

Temporal information (image acquisition dates) is logged for post-hoc drift analysis but is not used as a primary splitting criterion, since the hypothesis compares both model and radiologist on the same retrospective dataset, not on prospective deployment.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformations are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets, using training set statistics:
- Intensity normalization: zero mean, unit variance (computed from training set).
- Resampling: all volumes standardized to 256×256×32 voxels to normalize across scanner and acquisition protocol differences.
- Quality control: automated artifact detection and motion scoring applied uniformly; low-quality studies flagged (thresholds determined a priori on training data only).

No handcrafted radiomics features are engineered. The CNN operates end-to-end on preprocessed 3D volumes. Patient metadata (age, BMI, clinical history) are deliberately excluded from model inputs to ensure the comparison isolates image understanding.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** A 3D convolutional neural network (ResNet50-style architecture with 3D convolutions and pooling) takes 256×256×32 volumetric patches as input and outputs a binary logit (sigmoid-normalized to probability).

Hyperparameters (learning rate, regularization, depth, batch size) are tuned on the validation set via Bayesian optimization over a pre-specified search space. Early stopping halts training when validation AUC-ROC plateaus over 20 epochs; the model at the best validation checkpoint is retained.

**Baseline:** Radiologist assessments from the BCSC database: BI-RADS scores (1–5) converted to binary predictions (4–5 = malignant, 1–3 = benign). We use **consensus** of two independent radiologists (or the primary radiologist if consensus unavailable). This baseline is fair because radiologists have access to the same 3D images and no additional clinical information is provided to either radiologists or model.

A secondary baseline (logistic regression on tabular features) may decompose whether improvements are due to image understanding vs. model capacity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC)** on the held-out test set.

Justification: AUC-ROC is threshold-agnostic, invariant to class imbalance, and summarizes performance across all possible operating points. It allows fair comparison with radiologist performance without pre-specifying a single decision threshold. We report point estimate and 95% confidence interval (via 1,000-resample bootstrap).

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Sensitivity** at the model's predicted threshold that matches radiologists' test-set specificity.
- **Specificity** at the threshold matching radiologists' sensitivity.
- **Calibration error** (Expected Calibration Error, ECE): critical for clinical deployment; computed across 10 probability deciles.
- **Per-site AUC-ROC**: validates generalization across scanner types and protocols.
- **Per-demographic group AUC-ROC** (stratified by age decile, BMI quartile): checks for fairness and disparities.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (n≈1,000) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Early stopping monitors validation AUC-ROC; no final decisions are made until the test set is evaluated. The test set is held out and opened exactly once at the end of the experiment, after model selection is complete. No retraining, threshold adjustment, or post-hoc optimization occurs after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Demographic differences (age, BMI) are controlled via stratified splitting and measured via per-group secondary metrics. Institutional/equipment variability (scanner type, imaging protocol) is similarly addressed through site-stratified splitting and per-site secondary metrics. Radiologist variability is mitigated by using consensus assessment. Temporal drift is logged but not used for splitting, allowing post-hoc detection of performance divergence over the five-year span.

**Scope:** This experiment establishes whether a CNN can match or exceed radiologist accuracy *on retrospective BCSC data*. It does not establish prospective clinical utility, generalization to new institutions, or superiority in real-time deployment scenarios. The comparison isolates image-based prediction and does not account for clinical history, prior exams, or radiologist consultation workflows.
